name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ رَزَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ ۖ هَلْ مِن شُرَكَآئِكُم مَّن يَفْعَلُ مِن ذَٰلِكُم مِّن شَىْءٍ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٤٠)
```

```english
It is Allah who created you and then He provided for you, then He makes you die, then He will bring you to life. Is there anyone among your ‘partners’ who does anything of that kind? Immaculate is He and exalted above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]! (40)
```

```arabic
ظَهَرَ ٱلْفَسَادُ فِى ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِى ٱلنَّاسِ لِيُذِيقَهُم بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى عَمِلُوا۟ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٤١)
```

```english
Corruption has appeared in land and sea because of the doings of the people’s hands, that He may make them taste something of what they have done, so that they may come back. (41)
```

```arabic
قُلْ سِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّشْرِكِينَ (٤٢)
```

```english
Say, ‘Travel over the land, and see how was the fate of those who were before [you], most of whom were polytheists.’ (42)
```

```arabic
فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ ٱلْقَيِّمِ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِىَ يَوْمٌ لَّا مَرَدَّ لَهُۥ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصَّدَّعُونَ (٤٣)
```

```english
So set your heart on the upright religion, before there comes a day irrevocable from Allah. On that day they shall be split [into various groups]. (43)
```

```arabic
مَن كَفَرَ فَعَلَيْهِ كُفْرُهُۥ ۖ وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلِأَنفُسِهِمْ يَمْهَدُونَ (٤٤)
```

```english
Whoever is faithless shall face the consequences of his faithlessness, and those who act righteously only prepare for their own souls, (44)
```

```arabic
لِيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ مِن فَضْلِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٤٥)
```

```english
so that He may reward those who have faith and do righteous deeds out of His grace. Indeed, He does not like the faithless. (45)
```

```arabic
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦٓ أَن يُرْسِلَ ٱلرِّيَاحَ مُبَشِّرَٰتٍ وَلِيُذِيقَكُم مِّن رَّحْمَتِهِۦ وَلِتَجْرِىَ ٱلْفُلْكُ بِأَمْرِهِۦ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٤٦)
```

```english
And of His signs is that He sends the winds as bearers of good news and to let you taste of His mercy, and that the ships may sail by His command, and that you may seek of His bounty, and so that you may give [Him] thanks. (46)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ رُسُلًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِمْ فَجَآءُوهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا۟ ۖ وَكَانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنَا نَصْرُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٤٧)
```

```english
Certainly We sent apostles to their people before you, and they brought them manifest proofs. Then We took vengeance upon those who were guilty, and it was a must for Us to help the faithful. (47)
```

## (Commentary)

This is the second of the four sections in which He argues through His specific acts—or if you prefer, through the "Attributes of Act" (أسماء الأفعال)—for the invalidation of partners, the negation of their lordship (الربوبية) and divinity (الألوهية), and for the proof of the Resurrection (المعاد).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **اللَّهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ رَزَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ هَلْ مِنْ شُرَكَائِكُمْ مَنْ يَفْعَلُ مِنْ ذَلِكُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** "It is Allah who created you, then He provided for you, then He makes you die, then He will bring you to life. Is there anyone among your partners who does anything of that?" (30:40) etc. The Majestic Name (الله) is the subject (مبتدأ), and "the One who created you" (الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ) is its predicate (خبر). Similarly, His saying: "Is there anyone... who does" (مَنْ يَفْعَلُ) is a subject whose predicate is "among your partners" (مِنْ شُرَكَائِكُمْ), which precedes it. The interrogation here is one of denial (إنكاري). Other possibilities for the grammatical structure of the verse have also been mentioned.

The meaning is: Allah, the Glorified, is the One characterized by such-and-all attributes of divinity and lordship. Is there, then, among the deities whom you claim to be gods, anyone who does any of these things—meaning creation (الخلق), provision (الرزق), causing death (الإماتة), and bringing to life (الإحياء)? Since there is none among them who does any of that, then Allah, the Glorified, is your God and your Lord; there is no god but He.

Perhaps the reason for mentioning creation (الخلق) alongside provision (الرزق), and bringing to life (الإحياء) alongside causing death (الإماتة)—despite their repeated mention in previous arguments—is to indicate that provision is inseparable from creation. This means that some created things are called "provision" (رزق) in relation to others whose survival they sustain. Thus, provision is, in reality, a form of creation; so the One who creates the creation is the One who provides the provision.

Therefore, they have no right to say: "The Provider (الرازق), as well as the Giver of Life (المحيي) and the Bringer of Death (المميت), are some of our deities," as some of them might claim that the manager of the human world is one of the deities, and the manager of every affair of the world—whether good or evil—is another. However, they do not disagree that creation and origination (الإيجاد) are from Him, the Exalted, and that no one shares with Him in that. If this is conceded—and it is conceded that provision, for example, is a form of creation, and likewise all other affairs are inseparable from creation—then the matter returns to Him, the Exalted, just as creation does, and no role remains for their deities in any of these affairs.

Then, the Glorified declared His own transcendence (نزه سبحانه نفسه) above their polytheism, saying: **سُبْحَانَهُ وَتَعَالَى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** "Immaculate is He and exalted above [having] any partners they ascribe [to Him]!" (30:40).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **ظَهَرَ الْفَسادُ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ بِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِي النَّاسِ لِيُذِيقَهُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي عَمِلُوا لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ** "Corruption has appeared in land and sea because of what the hands of the people have earned, that He may let them taste some of what they have done, so that they may come back." (30:41). The verse, by its apparent wording, is general; it is not restricted to a particular time, place, or specific event. Thus, "land" (البر) and "sea" (البحر) are intended in their well-known meanings, encompassing the entire surface of the Earth.

"Corruption" (الفساد) refers to the manifest calamities and afflictions appearing in them, which affect various regions of the earth—such as earthquakes, the cessation of rain, droughts, contagious diseases, wars, raids, and the loss of security. In short, it includes everything that corrupts the sound order (النظام الصالح) prevailing in the terrestrial world, whether it stems from human choice or otherwise. All of these are manifest corruptions in the land or sea that disrupt the wholesome human life.

His saying: **بِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِي النَّاسِ** "because of what the hands of the people have earned" means due to the deeds they perform, whether polytheism (الشرك) or disobedience (المعصية). It has already been mentioned in the commentary on His saying: **وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ** "If the people of the towns had been faithful and God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth" (7:96), and also in the discussions on Prophethood in the second volume of this book, that there is a direct link between human actions and cosmic events; one is affected by the righteousness (صلاح) or corruption (فساد) of the other.

His saying: **لِيُذِيقَهُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي عَمِلُوا** "that He may let them taste some of what they have done"—the "lam" (ل) denotes the objective (للغاية). That is, what has appeared has appeared so that Allah may let them taste the evil consequence (وبال) of some of their wicked deeds; rather, so He may let them taste the very essence of what they have done, which has manifested in the form of an evil consequence. It was only "some" (بعض) of what they did because Allah, the Glorified, by His mercy, pardons some, as He said: **وَما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فَبِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُوا عَنْ كَثِيرٍ** "Whatever affliction may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many [an offense]." (42:30).

The verse looks toward the worldly consequence and the tasting of some of it, without regard for the consequence of deeds in the Hereafter. Thus, the claim that the meaning is "tasting the worldly consequence and delaying the otherworldly consequence until the Day of Resurrection" has no evidence. Perhaps this claimant assumed the implied meaning (تقدير) to be: "that He may let them taste some of the *recompense* for what they have done," whereas the implied meaning is: "that He may let them taste the recompense for *some* of what they have done." This is because what necessitates an implied noun—if anything does—is that what returns to them in the form of corruption is the recompense for their deeds, not the deeds themselves. Thus, what they are made to taste is the recompense for *some* of what they did, not *some* of the recompense for what they did.

His saying: **لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ** "so that they may come back" means He lets them taste what He lets them taste in the hope that they might return from their polytheism and disobedience to monotheism (التوحيد) and obedience (الطاعة).

The connection of this verse to the preceding one is that after He argued for monotheism in the previous verse and declared His transcendence above their polytheism, He pointed out in this verse the corruption on earth and the tasting of the evil consequences of sins that follow polytheism—which is itself a form of disobedience—explaining this in a general manner.

Exegetes have various strange interpretations for this verse. Some say "the land" (الأرض) refers to the land of Mecca. Others say "the land" (البر) refers to the deserts where no river flows, and "the sea" (البحر) refers to every village on the bank of a great river. Some say "the land" refers to the wilderness and tribal areas, while "the sea" refers to the coasts and cities (المدن) near the sea and rivers. Others say "the land" is the desert and "the sea" refers to fertile, green areas. Some say there is an omitted noun, the implied meaning being "in the land and the cities of the sea." Perhaps what led them to these claims is the report that the verse refers to the famine that occurred in Mecca following the prayer of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله against the Quraysh when they persisted in their disbelief and stubbornness. They wanted to apply the verse to the occasion of revelation (سبب النزول) and thus fell into such forced interpretations (التكلف).

Some also said that "corruption in the land" refers to the son of Adam killing his brother, and "in the sea" refers to the seizing of every ship by force. This is as you see [unfounded].

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قُلْ سِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلُ كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُشْرِكِينَ** "Say, ‘Travel over the land and observe how was the fate of those who were before [you]. Most of them were polytheists.’" (30:42). This is a command to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to order them to travel through the land and look at the ruins of those who came before, whose homes were destroyed, whose traces were erased, who perished to the last man, and whose lineage was cut off by various types of calamities and afflictions. Most of them were polytheists (مشركين), so Allah let them taste some of what they had done, so that those who take heed might learn and return to monotheism. Thus, the verse serves as a testimony to the content of the preceding verse.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ الْقَيِّمِ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ يَوْمٌ لا مَرَدَّ لَهُ مِنَ اللهِ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصَّدَّعُونَ** "So set your face toward the upright religion, before there comes a day from Allah which cannot be turned back. On that day they will be split into two groups." (30:43). This is a derivation (تفريع) from what preceded it; meaning, if polytheism and disbelief in the Truth are of such a nature and have an evil consequence that will overtake those who clothe themselves in them, then set your face toward the upright religion (الدين القيم) — *the true and stable faith that maintains the correct order of life*.

His saying: **مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ يَوْمٌ لا مَرَدَّ لَهُ مِنَ اللهِ** "before there comes a day from Allah which cannot be turned back" is connected to His saying: "So set" (فَأَقِمْ). The word "return" (المرد) is a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) meaning "turning back" (الرد), and it carries the sense of "that which turns back" (الراد). The day that cannot be turned back from Allah is the Day of Resurrection.

His saying: **يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصَّدَّعُونَ** "On that day they will be split into two groups" is originally (يتصدعون). "Splitting" (التصدع) originally refers to the breaking apart of the pieces of a vessel, then it was used for any kind of separation. It is said that the meaning here is their separation on that day into Paradise and the Fire.

It is also said that it refers to the separation of people as individuals, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **يَوْمَ يَكُونُ النَّاسُ كَالْفَراشِ الْمَبْثُوثِ** "the day when mankind will be like scattered moths" (101:4). Each view has its merit, but perhaps the most apparent meaning is the distinction between the two groups, as will follow.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **مَنْ كَفَرَ فَعَلَيْهِ كُفْرُهُ وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلِأَنْفُسِهِمْ يَمْهَدُونَ** "Whoever is faithless, his faithlessness will be to his own detriment, and those who act righteously, they prepare for their own souls" (30:44). It appears that this is an explanation for His saying in the previous verse: "they will be split" (يَتَفَرَّقُونَ). His saying: "Whoever is faithless, his faithlessness will be to his own detriment" (مَنْ كَفَرَ فَعَلَيْهِ كُفْرُهُ) means the evil consequence of his disbelief—assuming an implied noun—or the very essence of his disbelief which will turn against him as a fire in which he will abide forever. This is one of the two groups.

His saying: **وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلِأَنْفُسِهِمْ يَمْهَدُونَ** "and those who act righteously, they prepare for their own souls." To "prepare" (مهد) a bed is to spread it and make it comfortable. These are the other group who believed and performed righteous deeds (الصالحات). The recompense "they prepare for their own souls" (فَلِأَنْفُسِهِمْ يَمْهَدُونَ) is given in the plural form, considering the meaning, just as it was given in the singular in the previous conditional clause "to his own detriment" (فَعَلَيْهِ كُفْرُهُ), considering the literal wording. In the condition, He sufficed with mentioning righteous deeds without mentioning faith (الإيمان) alongside them, because a deed is only righteous through faith; moreover, faith is mentioned in the following verse.

The meaning is: those who performed righteous deeds—after having faith—prepare for their own souls that by which they shall live and upon which they shall settle.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْكافِرِينَ** "that He may reward those who have faith and do righteous deeds out of His grace. Indeed, He does not like the faithless." (30:45). Al-Raghib said: "Recompense" (الجزاء) is sufficiency and adequacy. Allah the Exalted said: **لا تَجْزِي نَفْسٌ عَنْ نَفْسٍ شَيْئاً** "no soul shall atone for another soul" (2:48), and He said: **لا يَجْزِي والِدٌ عَنْ وَلَدِهِ وَلا مَوْلُودٌ هُوَ جازٍ عَنْ والِدِهِ شَيْئاً** "no father shall atone for his child, nor shall any child atone for his father" (31:33). Recompense is that which provides sufficiency in exchange—if good, then good; if evil, then evil. It is said: "I recompensed him (جزيته) such-and-such." [End of quote].

His saying: **لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ** "that He may reward those who have faith and do righteous deeds out of His grace"—the "lam" (ل) denotes the objective. There is no contradiction between counting what He gives them as a "reward" (جزاء)—which carries the meaning of exchange—and counting it as being from His "grace" (فضل)—which carries the meaning of a lack of entitlement. This is because they themselves, and whatever deeds they produce, are the absolute property of Allah, the Glorified. Thus, they own nothing for themselves such that they would be entitled to a wage. Where is servanthood (العبودية) in relation to ownership and entitlement? Therefore, what they are given as a reward is grace without entitlement.

However, the Glorified, by His grace and mercy, considered them to have ownership of their deeds, even while He owns them and owns their deeds. Thus, He established for them a right which they deserve, and He made what they attain of Paradise and proximity (الزلفى) a wage in exchange for their deeds. This established right is also another grace from Him, the Glorified.

The source of this is His love for them. Because they loved their Lord, they set their faces toward the upright religion and followed the Messenger in what he called toward; so Allah loved them, as He said: **قُلْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ اللهَ فَاتَّبِعُونِي يُحْبِبْكُمُ اللهُ** "Say, ‘If you love Allah, then follow me; Allah will love you’" (3:31).

For this reason, the verse counts what Allah gives them of reward as "recompense" (جزاء), which carries the meaning of exchange and substitution, and counts it as being from His "grace" (فضل), considering that this very exchange and substitution is a grace from Him, the Glorified. Its source is His love for them, as hinted at by the conclusion of the verse: **إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْكافِرِينَ** "Indeed, He does not like the faithless."

From here it becomes clear that His saying: "Indeed, He does not like the faithless" provides the justification (التعليل) for both the negative and positive aspects. That is, He, the Exalted, singles out the believers who do righteous deeds for this grace and deprives the disbelievers of it, because He loves the former and does not love the latter.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَمِنْ آياتِهِ أَنْ يُرْسِلَ الرِّياحَ مُبَشِّراتٍ وَلِيُذِيقَكُمْ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ وَلِتَجْرِيَ الْفُلْكُ بِأَمْرِهِ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَلَعلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ** "And of His signs is that He sends the winds as bearers of good news and to let you taste of His mercy, and that the ships may sail by His command, and that you may seek of His grace, and so that you may give thanks." (30:46). The meaning of the winds being "bearers of good news" (مبشرات) is their announcing the coming of rain when they blow shortly before its descent.

His saying: **وَلِيُذِيقَكُمْ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ** "and to let you taste of His mercy" is conjoined to the position of "bearers of good news" because of the sense of justification it contains. The implied meaning is: He sends the winds to bring you good news and to let you taste of His mercy. "Tasting of mercy" refers to being touched by the various types of blessings resulting from the blowing of winds, such as the pollination of trees, the dispelling of foul odors, the purification of the air, and other things encompassed by the generality of the sentence.

His saying: **وَلِتَجْرِيَ الْفُلْكُ بِأَمْرِهِ** "and that the ships may sail by His command" refers to the movement and blowing of the winds. His saying: **وَلِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ** "and that you may seek of His grace" means to seek His provision (الرزق), which is from His grace.

His saying: **وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ** "and so that you may give thanks" is a spiritual objective (غاية معنوية), just as the previously mentioned objectives are formal objectives (غايات صورية). "Gratitude" (الشكر) — *the act of using a blessing in a manner that acknowledges the benefactor or praising Him verbally by mentioning His favor* — ultimately corresponds to His worship (العبادة). For this reason, it was introduced with "perhaps" (لعل), which denotes hope, as spiritual and conventional objectives may sometimes fail to be realized.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ رُسُلاً إِلى قَوْمِهِمْ فَجاؤُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَانْتَقَمْنا مِنَ الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا وَكانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** "Certainly We sent messengers before you to their people, and they brought them manifest proofs; then We took vengeance upon those who were guilty, and it was a must for Us to help the faithful." (30:47). Al-Raghib said: The root of "crime" (الجرم) is the picking of fruit from the tree... and "he committed a crime" (أجرم) means he became a possessor of crime, similar to "it bore fruit" (أثمر). This was then borrowed for every disliked acquisition, and it is hardly ever used in common speech for a praiseworthy gain. [End of quote].

The verse is like a parenthetical statement, as if it were intended to explain that the believers have a right upon their Lord, which is His helping them in this world and the Hereafter, and part of that is taking vengeance upon the guilty (المجرمين). This right is established by Him, the Exalted, for them upon Himself; thus, it does not entail the problematic notion of Him being overcome in Himself or being subdued and judged by another.

His saying: **فَانْتَقَمْنا مِنَ الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا** "then We took vengeance upon those who were guilty"—the "fa" (الفاء) is elided (فصيحة); meaning: some of them believed and others committed crimes, so We took vengeance upon the guilty and it was a right upon Us to help the believers by saving them from the punishment and destroying their opponents. The verse contains some indication that the vengeance upon the guilty is for the sake of the believers, as it is part of the "help" (النصر).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **ظَهَرَ الْفَسادُ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ بِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِي النَّاسِ** "Corruption has appeared in land and sea because of what the hands of the people have earned," he said: "In the land, it is the corruption of animals when it does not rain—and likewise the destruction of sea creatures because of that."

Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "The life of sea creatures is through rain—so when the rain is withheld, corruption appears in the land and the sea, and that is when sins and acts of disobedience increase."

**I say**: This is a form of conceptual application (الجري).

In *Rawdat al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Abi al-Rabi' al-Shami, who said: "I asked Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **قُلْ سِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلُ** 'Say, "Travel over the land and observe how was the fate of those who were before [you]."' He replied: 'He meant by that: Look into the Qur'an—and observe how was the fate of those who were before you.'"

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying: **وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلِأَنْفُسِهِمْ يَمْهَدُونَ** "and those who act righteously, they prepare for their own souls," Mansur ibn Hazim narrated from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) who said: "Indeed, the righteous deed precedes its doer to Paradise—so it prepares for him just as the servant of one of you prepares his bed."

In the same source, a narration came from Umm al-Darda' that she said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) saying: 'There is no man who defends the honor of his brother except that it becomes a right upon Allah to turn away from him the fire of Hell on the Day of Resurrection.' Then he recited: **وَكانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** 'and it was a must for Us to help the faithful.'"

**I say**: It was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh, from Abu al-Darda'.
